## [15:08 - 18:10]

And then with level three, they're aware of the problem and the solution. And here's a quick refresher. So the 80-20 principle says that we basically, in everything we do, 20% of the effort drives 80% of the results. And 80% of the effort drives 20% of the results. So really our results come from these small little inputs. But, you know, and most of our time is wasted. Most of our efforts, most of our money, most of our energy, most of everything that we do is wasted. And only a very small amount of it is responsible for bringing most of the results. And here's some examples of 80-20 in nature just to, you know, refresh your memory on this. So it's fascinating. We see this exponential distribution everywhere. So 80% of the planet is water and 20% of the planet is land.

## [33:23 - 36:27]

And what you're really using to do this is 80-20. Look at the e-score and apply 80-20. What is the top 20% of the efficient pieces of your offer? And then what's the bottom 80% of the things in your offer? You really want to start trimming from that 80%. You want to be able to remove a whole bunch of stuff from your offer. Because like I said, most consultants, they get into business with a nice lean, clean and focused offer and over time they get pulled in different directions by all of these different clients. And they see these different things and they're like, oh yeah, I'll provide that or oh yeah, I'll provide that to my clients too. And their whole business and their offer gets totally messed up over time. And that's why we've got this process here so that you can really work through and drill down and then cut the fat from your offer. And you want to do this process really every 90 days. You want to work through and make sure your offer is always being refined and it's always being cleaned up. And then once you're done with this particular tab here, the 80-20 offer refinement, you want to go to the next tab which is your new offer. So here, all we're doing is we're removing the things that we don't want in our offer. So for example, if I have 12 things here, and I'm going to cut it down to six, well, you just select the six at the bottom or you select the six that you're going to remove and you just delete them. And don't worry, it's not going to delete them from the previous tabs. So instead of, you know, we're going to say, are we going to remove it? Yes or no? Well, whatever our decision is here, we're just going to go ahead and remove it here.

## [39:31 - 42:34]

And then once we've removed it, now you're looking at your new offer. This is your offer after all the fat's being trimmed off it. And now what you might start to do is you might think, well, you know, this particular thing here, now that I'm thinking about it, I might want to make a small adjustment or refinement to this particular piece of my offer. Well, you can type in any adjustments or refinements you want to make here and any notes here. And this final tab called your new offer, this is going to show you what your offer is. And this process is extremely powerful for really taking your offer, drilling it down, cutting the fat off it, and then crafting, you know, a new, more honed and refined one. And like I said, this is a process you want to work through pretty much every 90 days or at least once a year because your offer is going to get bloated over time and it's really going to lose its power. And when you keep doing this process to it over and over and over again, what you'll find is your offer keeps getting better and better and better. So this is a very powerful process to use for all consultants and coaches and service providers. So the next section is called learning to think like a billionaire with the first principles method. So what is the first principles method? Well, most people reason by analogy and then a very rare small group of people reason by first principles. And it's pretty much like billionaires that use first principles and then the rest of the world just uses analogy. So I'll explain the two to you right now. So we'll start with reasoning by analogy. So reasoning by analogy is when you solve an argument by relating it to a common saying, belief, trend, or the way it's being done now or has been done before, or a particular example you can think of. So this is how you reason. So if you see an argument which is like, you should do this because of this. Well, you're going to look at that argument and then you're going to use your reasoning mechanism in your brain and most people's reasoning mechanism is what is the common belief? What is everyone out there thinking and saying right now? What are other people doing and what are some other examples I've seen? It's basically just not even doing your own thinking. It's basically just defaulting to what everyone else is doing. That's reasoning by analogy. Now on the other side, there's reasoning by first principles. And reasoning by first principles is when you boil down an argument to its most fundamental truths

## [42:34 - 45:37]

and then build it up from there using deductive logic. And don't worry if this sounds a little bit confusing right now. I've got some examples which I'm going to show you that will really help bring clarity to this thing. So when we reason by analogy, we're really not doing any of our own thinking. We're just comparing what we're deciding to what other people have done. So we're never really thinking for ourselves. But when we're reasoning with first principles, we're really boiling the argument right down. We're not caring about what anyone else has said or what anyone else has done. We're just trying to really boil the argument down to the most fundamental truth in it and then start building up from there. And like I said, this is how billionaires think and this is how pretty much everyone else in the world thinks. And so this is a very powerful method when you learn how to use it. So here's some examples to help really cement your understanding. So example number one, using Snapchat for your business. So we'll start with reasoning by analogy. So the premise would be you should use Snapchat for your business because everybody is using it and it's the next big thing. That might be an argument that someone's making. You might hear someone say that. I'm sure everyone's heard that. Now the typical person will do their thinking like this. This is how they will reason. They'll reason by analogy. Well, Gary Vaynerchuk said to use Snapchat for your business and Gary is using Snapchat for his business and So has Kim Kardashian and all of my friends. Then the conclusion, therefore I should use Snapchat for my business. That's how most people think. As shocking as that is, that's how they do it. Now here's the other way to do it. Reasoning by first principles. So the premise is the same. You should use Snapchat for your business because everybody is using it and it's the next big thing. Well here's some reasoning with first principles. Snapchat is a social media platform. Compared to Facebook ads, Snapchat doesn't provide as much results to businesses. I'm a business and I want results. If I use Snapchat, then it will distract me from using Facebook ads and that would decrease my results. Conclusion, therefore I shouldn't use Snapchat for my business. So you see the difference here. On one side with the analogy, we just look at famous people and friends that are doing it and then on the other side, we boil it down and we really just look at it not as who's using it, but we just boil it down to its most fundamental truth. And then we start building up from there. Well if A is true, then B is true. If A and B are true, then C is true. That's kind of how we work up. And so this is really, really key to learn as a business owner.

## [45:37 - 48:37]

It'll help you make decisions with much more clarity. Now here's a second example. Is Donald Trump a racist? So reasoning by analogy. So the premise, someone could say, well Donald Trump is a racist because he said a racist thing. Now the reasoning, if we were reasoning by analogy, someone would say, well Donald Trump said a racist thing. Everybody thinks and says he's a racist and he even looks like a racist. Therefore Donald Trump is a racist. That's reasoning by analogy. Then reasoning by first principles. Well, the premise is the same. Donald Trump is a racist because he said a racist thing. Then the reasoning, well Donald Trump said a racist thing. Everybody in the world has said a racist thing at one point in their life. But everyone in the world isn't a racist. Therefore Donald Trump isn't a racist. You see the key difference here. We boil it down and we use, if A is true, then B is true and we kind of build up like that. Now I don't actually know if he is or isn't. I don't know him, never met him. So I can't say whether he actually is or isn't. But what I do know is if we're to assess the argument, like Donald Trump is a racist because he said a racist thing, if we're just purely to isolate that and really analyse that, well, using first principles we get the exact opposite result. And most of the time in business, if you use the first principles method, you're going to get a different result to what everyone else thinks. And it's going to be a much more accurate one. Now here's the third example. Sam Ovens' Consulting Program is a scam. So reasoning by analogy. So the premise. Well Sam Ovens' Consulting Program is a scam because you can find all of the information within it on the internet in books and through trial and error. That's the premise. Then the reasoning, if we're reasoning by analogy. Well I could learn everything in Sam Ovens' Consulting Program by searching the internet, reading books and practicing with trial and error. And my friend said he was a scammer too. Therefore Sam Ovens' Program is a scam. Alright. Now here's first principles. Premise. Sam Ovens' Consulting Program is a scam because you can find all of the information within it on the internet in books and through trial and error. Reasoning. With first principles this time. Well Sam Ovens' Consulting Program is made up of components that can be found elsewhere for cheap. And iPhone and house are made up of materials that can be found in the ground for cheap. iPhones and houses aren't scams because the combination of materials provides value over combining them on your own. And Sam Ovens' Program provides value over combining the components on your own. Therefore Sam Ovens' Program isn't a scam. Alright. You see the difference in thinking here.

One-on-one time and reports isn't more customers for them. Providing one-on-one calls and reports would distract me from providing more customers to them. Conclusion. because one-on-one calls and reports isn't results and all our clients want are results.
